subject: Physics
q: A particle P is projected from a point on the surface of smooth inclined plane (see figure). It is projected perpendicular to the plane. Simultaneously, another particle Q is released on the smooth inclined plane from the same position. P and Q collide after $t = 4$ seconds. The speed of projection of P is
question: A particle P is projected from a point on the surface of smooth inclined plane (see figure). It is projected perpendicular to the plane. Simultaneously, another particle Q is released on the smooth inclined plane from the same position. P and Q collide after $t = 4$ seconds. The speed of projection of P is
solution:
Let assume a coordinate system where the x-axis is along the incline (downwards) and the y-axis is perpendicular to the incline.

The only force acting on the particles is gravity (g), acting vertically downwards. On resolving this acceleration this into components relative to assumed coordinate system:

Acceleration perpendicular to the plane is $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}}=-\mathrm{g} \cos 60^{\circ}$

Acceleration parallel to the plane is $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{g} \sin 60^{\circ}$

Particle $Q$ is released from rest, initial velocity is $u_{Q x}=0, u_{Q y}=0$

Using the second equation of motion $\left(s=u t+\frac{1}{2} a t^2\right)$ :

$$ \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{Q}}=0+\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{~g} \sin 60^{\circ}\right) \mathrm{t}^2 $$

Particle P is projected with a speed u perpendicular to the plane.

So, initial velocity components are $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{Px}}=0, \mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{Py}}=\mathrm{u}$

The particle goes up and comes back to the plane. For a collision to occur on the plane, the displacement of P in the y -direction must be zero at time $\mathrm{t}=4 \mathrm{~s}$.

$$ \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{Py}} \mathrm{t}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{t}^2 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $0=\mathrm{u}(\mathrm{t})+\frac{1}{2}\left(-\mathrm{g} \cos 60^{\circ}\right) \mathrm{t}^2$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{u}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} \cos 60^{\circ} \cdot \mathrm{t}$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{u}=\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

Therefore, the speed of projection of P is $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.

Hence, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q: The horizontal range of a projectile is R and the maximum height attained by the projectile is H. A strong wind now begins to blow in the direction of motion of the projectile giving it a horizontal acceleration equal to $\frac{g}{2}$. Under the same conditions of projection, the horizontal range of projectile is
question: The horizontal range of a projectile is R and the maximum height attained by the projectile is H. A strong wind now begins to blow in the direction of motion of the projectile giving it a horizontal acceleration equal to $\frac{g}{2}$. Under the same conditions of projection, the horizontal range of projectile is
solution:
Let the initial velocity be u and the angle of projection be $\theta$.

On resolving the initial velocity, $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{u} \cos \theta$ and $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{u} \sin \theta$

Vertical acceleration is $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}}=-\mathrm{g}$ and horizontal acceleration is $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{x}}=0$

Using $\mathrm{s}_{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{t}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{t}^2$ for the whole flight $\left(\mathrm{s}_{\mathrm{y}}=0\right)$ :

$$ 0=(\mathrm{u} \sin \theta) \mathrm{T}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gT}^2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{~T}=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{~g}} $$

Using $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}^2=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}}^2+$ 2as at the peak $\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}=0\right)$ :

$$ 0=(u \sin \theta)^2-2 g H \Rightarrow H=\frac{u^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 g} \ldots (i)$$

Along horizontal,

$$ \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{x}} \times \mathrm{T}=(\mathrm{u} \cos \theta)\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{~g}}\right)=\frac{2 \mathrm{u}^2 \sin \theta \cos \theta}{\mathrm{~g}} \ldots (ii)$$

When the wind provides a horizontal acceleration $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{\mathrm{g}}{2}$ in the direction of motion. The vertical motion remains unchanged, so the time of flight (T) remains the same.

Using the second equation of motion for the horizontal direction :

$$ \mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{~T}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{~T}^2 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{R}^{\prime}=(\mathrm{u} \cos \theta) \mathrm{T}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{~g}}{2}\right) \mathrm{T}^2 $$

Substituting $\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$ into the new range equation :

$$ \mathrm{R}^{\prime}=(\mathrm{u} \cos \theta)\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{~g}}\right)+\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~g}\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{u} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{~g}}\right)^2 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\mathrm{R}+\frac{\mathrm{g}}{4}\left(\frac{4 \mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{\mathrm{~g}^2}\right)$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\mathrm{R}+\frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{\mathrm{~g}} $$

Using expression for H , where $\mathrm{H}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{u}^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{\mathrm{~g}}=2 \mathrm{H}$.

Substitute this into the $\mathrm{R}^{\prime}$ equation:

$$ \mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\mathrm{R}+2 \mathrm{H} $$

Therefore, the horizontal range of projectile is $\mathrm{R}+2 \mathrm{H}$.

Hence, the correct option is (D).

subject: Physics
q: A particle is projected at an angle of $60^\circ$ above the horizontal with a speed of $10\ \mathrm{m/s}$. After sometime the direction of its velocity makes an angle of $30^\circ$ above the horizontal. The speed of the particle at this instant is
question: A particle is projected at an angle of $60^\circ$ above the horizontal with a speed of $10\ \mathrm{m/s}$. After sometime the direction of its velocity makes an angle of $30^\circ$ above the horizontal. The speed of the particle at this instant is
solution:
For the projectile motion,

The vertical acceleration is $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}}=-\mathrm{g}$

The horizontal acceleration is $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{x}}=0$

So, the horizontal component of its velocity ( $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}$ ) remains constant throughout the entire flight of time.

The particle is projected with an initial speed $u=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at an angle $\theta=60^{\circ}$ to the horizontal.

The horizontal component of this velocity is $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{u} \cos \theta=10 \cos \left(60^{\circ}\right)$

$$ \mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{x}}=10 \times \frac{1}{2}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

Let the new speed of the particle at the instant be v . At this moment, its velocity vector makes an angle $\alpha= 30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal.

The horizontal component of this new velocity is:

$$V_x = v \cos \alpha = v \cos(30^\circ)$$

$$V_x = v \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

Since horizontal velocity does not change ($V_x = u_x$):

$$v \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 5$$

$$v = 5 \times 2\sqrt{3}$$

$$v = 10\sqrt{3}\ \text{m/s}$$

Therefore, the speed of the particle at that instant is $10\sqrt{3}\ \text{m/s}$. Thus, the correct option is (D).

subject: Physics
q: A particle is projected from the ground at an angle of $\theta$ with the horizontal with an initial speed of $u$. Time after which velocity vector of the projectile is perpendicular to the initial velocity is
question: A particle is projected from the ground at an angle of $\theta$ with the horizontal with an initial speed of $u$. Time after which velocity vector of the projectile is perpendicular to the initial velocity is
solution:
The particle is projected with a speed $u$ at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal plane.

Resolving initial velocity vector into its components

$$\vec{u} = u_x\hat{i} + u_y\hat{j}$$

$$\vec{u} = (u\cos\theta)\hat{i} + (u\sin\theta)\hat{j}$$

The acceleration acting on the projectile during its flight is gravity, which acts vertically downwards:

$$\vec{a} = 0\hat{i} - g\hat{j} = -g\hat{j}$$

As the acceleration is constant, so we can use the equation of motion to find the final velocity ($\vec{v}$) at any general instant $t$

$$\vec{v} = \vec{u} + \vec{a}t$$

$$\vec{v} = \vec{u} - gt\hat{j}$$

$$\vec{v} = (u\cos\theta)\hat{i} + (u\sin\theta - gt)\hat{j}$$

It is given that at some time $t$, the final velocity vector $\vec{v}$ becomes perpendicular to the initial velocity vector $\vec{u}$.

The dot product (scalar product) of any two non-zero vectors that are mutually perpendicular is zero:

$$\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = 0$$

$$[(u \cos \theta)\hat{i} + (u \sin \theta)\hat{j}] \cdot [(u \cos \theta)\hat{i} + (u \sin \theta - gt)\hat{j}] = 0$$

$$(u \cos \theta)(u \cos \theta) + (u \sin \theta)(u \sin \theta - gt) = 0$$

$$u^2 \cos^2 \theta + u^2 \sin^2 \theta - ugt \sin \theta = 0$$

$$u^2 (\cos^2 \theta + \sin^2 \theta) - ugt \sin \theta = 0$$

$$u^2 (1) - ugt \sin \theta = 0$$

$$u^2 = ugt \sin \theta$$

Since the initial velocity $u \ne 0$,

$$u = gt \sin \theta$$

$$t = \frac{u}{g \sin \theta}$$

Therefore, the time after which the velocity vector becomes perpendicular to the initial velocity is $\frac{u}{g \sin \theta}$. Thus,

the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q: In fig, the angle of inclination of the inclined plane is $30^{\circ}$. Find the horizontal velocity $v_0$ so that the particle hits the inclined plane perpendicularly.
question: In fig, the angle of inclination of the inclined plane is $30^{\circ}$. Find the horizontal velocity $v_0$ so that the particle hits the inclined plane perpendicularly.
solution:
The horizontal component of velocity $\left(v_x=v_0\right)$ remains constant throughout the flight. The vertical component $\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}\right)$ increases due to gravity.

If the particle hits an incline of angle $30^{\circ}$ perpendicularly, its velocity vector $\vec{v}$ must be perpendicular to the surface. This means the angle that the velocity vector makes with the horizontal is $(90^{\circ} - 30^ {\circ} = 60^{\circ})$.

Let the point of projection be $(0, H)$ and the impact point be $(x, h)$.

The point of impact $(x, y)$ must lie on the inclined plane.

The equation of the plane taking the foot of the tower as origin is:

$$y = x \tan 30^{\circ} \Rightarrow y = \frac{x}{\sqrt{3}}$$

In time $t$, the horizontal displacement is $x = v_0\, t$.

Using equation of motion along vertical direction,

$$y = H - h = \frac{1}{2} g\, t^2$$

Vertical velocity at impact is $v_y = gt$

Since the impact is perpendicular to the $30^{\circ}$ incline:

$$\tan 60^ {\circ} = \frac{v_y}{v_x}$$

$$\frac{gt}{v_0} = \sqrt{3} \Rightarrow t = \frac{v_0\sqrt{3}}{g} \ldots (i)$$

The height from the ground to the impact point is h. Using the triangle formed by the incline:

$$\tan 30^\circ = \frac{h}{x} \Rightarrow h = x \tan 30^\circ = (v_0\, t)\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$

Substituting t from equation (i):

$$h = v_0 \left(\frac{v_0\sqrt{3}}{g}\right)\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{v_0^2}{g} \ldots \text{(ii)}$$

We know the total height H is the sum of the vertical fall and the height of the impact point:

$$H = (H - h) + h$$

$$H = \frac{1}{2} g\, t^2 + h$$

Substituting t from equation 1 and h from equation (ii):

$$H = \frac{1}{2} g \left(\frac{v_0\sqrt{3}}{g}\right)^2 + \frac{v_0^2}{g}$$

$$H = \frac{1}{2} g \left(\frac{3v_0^2}{g^2}\right) + \frac{v_0^2}{g}$$

$$H = \frac{3v_0^2}{2g} + \frac{v_0^2}{g} = \frac{3v_0^2 + 2v_0^2}{2g}$$

$$H = \frac{5v_0^2}{2g}$$

$$v_0^2 = \frac{2gH}{5}$$

$$v_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2gH}{5}}$$

Therefore, the required horizontal velocity is $\sqrt{\frac{2gH}{5}}$. Hence, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q: A ball is projected upwards from the foot of a tower. The ball crosses the top of the tower twice after an interval of 6 s and the ball reaches the ground after 12 s. The height of the tower is $(g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2)$
question: A ball is projected upwards from the foot of a tower. The ball crosses the top of the tower twice after an interval of 6 s and the ball reaches the ground after 12 s. The height of the tower is $(g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2)$
solution:
Let the ball be projected vertically upward from the ground (foot of the tower) with an initial velocity $u$. The acceleration throughout the motion is due to gravity acting downwards $\mathrm{a}=-\mathrm{g}=-10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$.

The position (height $y$) at any time $t$ relative to the ground is

$$y(t) = ut - \frac{1}{2}gt^2$$

When the ball returns to the ground, its net displacement is zero ($y = 0$). It is given that the time after which the ball returns to the ground is $t = 12\ \text{s}$:

$$0 = u(12) - \frac{1}{2}g(12)^2$$

$$12u = \frac{1}{2}(10)(144)$$

$$12u = 720 \Rightarrow u = 60\ \text{m/s}$$

So, the initial projection speed from the ground is 60 m/s.

Let H be the height of the tower. The ball crosses this height H twice, once on its way up at time $t_1$, and once on its way down at time $t_2$.

$$H = ut - \frac{1}{2}gt^2 \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}gt^2 - ut + H = 0$$

$$5t^2 - 60t + H = 0$$

$$t^2 - 12t + \frac{H}{5} = 0$$

Using the solution of quadratic equations $(At^2 + Bt + C = 0)$, the difference between the roots is given by:

$$t_2 - t_1 = \frac{\sqrt{B^2 - 4AC}}{A}$$

It is given that the time interval between these two crossings is 6 s, so $t_2 - t_1 = 6$.

Here, $A = 1$, $B = -12$, and $C = \frac{H}{5}$.

$$6 = \frac{\sqrt{(-12)^2 - 4(1)\left(\frac{H}{5}\right)}}{1}$$

$$6 = \sqrt{144 - \frac{4H}{5}}$$

$$36 = 144 - \frac{4H}{5}$$

$$\frac{4H}{5} = 144 - 36$$

$$\frac{4H}{5} = 108$$

$$4H = 108 \times 5$$

$$4H = 540$$

$$H = \frac{540}{4} = 135\ \text{m}$$

Therefore, the height of the tower is 135 m. Thus, the correct option is (B).

subject: Physics
q: In projectile motion, the modulus of rate of change of speed
question: In projectile motion, the modulus of rate of change of speed
solution:
The velocity ($\vec{v}$) is a vector quantity that gives the rate of change of position a particle is covering and its

direction, but the speed ($v$) is a scalar quantity representing the absolute magnitude of the velocity vector,

$$v = |\vec{v}|$$

Rate of change of velocity ($\dfrac{d\vec{v}}{dt}$) is the total acceleration vector ($\vec{a}$), which is the constant downward acceleration

due to gravity ($\vec{g}$) for projectile motion.

Modulus of rate of change of speed ($\left|\dfrac{dv}{dt}\right|$) represents the absolute magnitude of the tangential acceleration ($a_t$).

It measures the rate at which speed is changing irrespective of direction.

Let a particle be projected with an initial velocity vector $\vec{u} = u_x\hat{i} + u_y\hat{j}$.

Because there are zero horizontal forces, the horizontal velocity component remains constant at

$$v_x = u_x$$

Under constant downward gravity $g$, the vertical velocity at any time $t$ is

$$v_y = u_y - gt$$

The instantaneous speed $v$ is the magnitude of these components:

$$v = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2}$$

To find the rate of change of speed ($\dfrac{dv}{dt}$), we need to differentiate this with respect to time $t$

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2}\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2}} \times \frac{d}{dt}(v_x^2 + v_y^2)$$

Since $v_x$ is constant, its time derivative is zero ($\dfrac{d}{dt}(v_x^2) = 0$).

For the vertical term,

$$\frac{d}{dt}(v_y^2) = 2v_y \cdot \frac{dv_y}{dt} = 2v_y(-g) = -2gv_y.$$

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = -\frac{2gv_y}{2\sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2}} = -\frac{gv_y}{\sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2}}$$

Since $\sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2} = v$, the rate of change of speed is:

$$\frac{dv}{dt}=-g\left(\frac{v_y}{v}\right)$$

$$\left|\frac{dv}{dt}\right|=g\left|\frac{v_y}{v}\right|$$

As the projectile goes up toward its highest point, the vertical velocity component $v_y$ decreases continuously until it reaches zero at the maximum height. Meanwhile, the total speed $v$ is also decreasing, but it never reaches zero because the horizontal component $v_x$ is constant and non-zero.

Because $v_y$ becomes $0$ while $v$ approaches a non-zero minimum $(v_x)$, the fraction $\dfrac{|v_y|}{v}$ decreases until it becomes $0$ at the top. So, the modulus $\left|\dfrac{dv}{dt}\right|$ decreases.

At the highest point, $v_y=0$, so $\left|\dfrac{dv}{dt}\right|=0$.

As the particle drops from the maximum height, $v_y$ increases in the negative direction, meaning its absolute value $|v_y|$ increases from $0$. The total speed $v$ also begins to increase again.

As the particle continues falling, $v_y$ increases making the modulus $\left|\dfrac{dv}{dt}\right|$ closer to $1$. Hence, the modulus $\left|\dfrac{dv}{dt}\right|$ increases.

Hence, the value of $\left|\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right|$ first decreases until it becomes zero at the peak, and then increases during the descent. Therefore, the modulus of the rate of change of speed first decreases then increases.

Thus, the correct option is (C).

subject: Physics
q: A particle is projected vertically upwards from a point A on the ground. It takes time $t_1$ to reach a point B, but it still continues to move up. If it takes further $t_2$ time to reach the ground from point B. Then height of point B from the ground is
question: A particle is projected vertically upwards from a point A on the ground. It takes time $t_1$ to reach a point B, but it still continues to move up. If it takes further $t_2$ time to reach the ground from point B. Then height of point B from the ground is
solution:
Let the ground be the reference position $(y=0)$ and the upward direction be positive.

Let the initial launch velocity from point A be u , moving under constant downward gravity g .

The particle moves upward from ground point A to point B in a time interval equal to $\mathrm{t}_1$.

From point B, it continues ascending to its highest point, turns back, and falls down to the ground.

It is given that this entire return journey from B back to the ground takes a further time interval equal to $\mathrm{t}_2$.

Therefore, the total time of flight (T) from the moment of projection to returning to the ground is the sum of these two consecutive intervals:

$$ \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{t}_1+\mathrm{t}_2 $$

The height equation as a function of time is

$$ \mathrm{y}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{ut}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gt}^2 $$

When the particle returns to the ground, its height returns to zero $(y=0)$ at $t=T$ :

$$ 0=\mathrm{uT}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gT}^2 $$

Since $\mathrm{T} \neq 0$,

$$ \mathrm{u}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gT} $$

Substituting $\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{t}_1+\mathrm{t}_2$ :

$$ \mathrm{u}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g}\left(\mathrm{t}_1+\mathrm{t}_2\right) $$

Point B is reached at time $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}_1$.

Let the height of point B from the ground be h .

Substituting $t=t_1$ into general height equation:

$$h=u(t_1)-\frac{1}{2}g(t_1)^2$$

$$h=\left[\frac{1}{2}g(t_1+t_2)\right]t_1-\frac{1}{2}gt_1^2$$

$$h=\frac{1}{2}gt_1^2+\frac{1}{2}gt_1t_2-\frac{1}{2}gt_1^2$$

$$h=\frac{1}{2}gt_1t_2$$

Therefore, the height of point B from the ground is $\frac{1}{2}gt_1t_2$. Thus, the correct option is (D).

subject: Physics
q:
Two particles A and B are placed in gravity free space at (0, 0, 0) m and (30, 0, 0) m respectively.

Particle A is projected with a velocity $(5\hat{i} + 10\hat{j} + 5\hat{k}) \text{ ms}^{-1}$, while particle B is projected with a velocity $(10\hat{i} + 5\hat{j} + 5\hat{k}) \text{ ms}^{-1}$ simultaneously. Then
question:
Two particles A and B are placed in gravity free space at (0, 0, 0) m and (30, 0, 0) m respectively.

Particle A is projected with a velocity $(5\hat{i} + 10\hat{j} + 5\hat{k}) \text{ ms}^{-1}$, while particle B is projected with a velocity $(10\hat{i} + 5\hat{j} + 5\hat{k}) \text{ ms}^{-1}$ simultaneously. Then
solution:
Because the particles are moving in a gravity-free space, no external forces act upon them. By Newton's First Law, their acceleration vectors are zero.

The particle moving at a constant velocity is $\vec{v} = \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt}$:

$$\vec{r}(t) = \vec{r}_0 + \vec{v}t$$

For Particle A :

The initial position is $\vec{r}_A(0) = 0\hat{i} + 0\hat{j} + 0\hat{k}$

The constant velocity is $\vec{v}_A = 5\hat{i} + 10\hat{j} + 5\hat{k}$

So, the position vector of particle at any time $t$ is

$$\vec{r}_A(t) = (5t)\hat{i} + (10t)\hat{j} + (5t)\hat{k}$$

For Particle B:

The initial position is $\vec{r}_B(0) = 30\hat{i} + 0\hat{j} + 0\hat{k}$

The constant velocity is $\vec{v}_B = 10\hat{i} + 5\hat{j} + 5\hat{k}$

So, the position vector of particle at any time $t$ is

$$\vec{r}_B(t) = (30 + 10t)\hat{i} + (5t)\hat{j} + (5t)\hat{k}$$

For a collision to take place, both particles must be at the exact same point in space at the exact same instant in time $t$. Therefore:

$$\vec{r}_A(t) = \vec{r}_B(t)$$

The x-component is $5t = 30 + 10t \Rightarrow 5t - 10t = 30 \Rightarrow -5t = 30 \Rightarrow t = -6\ \mathrm{s}$

A negative time value ($t = -6\ \mathrm{s}$) means the particles would have collided 6 seconds before the particles were launched which is not possible because the particles were projected simultaneously at $t = 0$.

The y-component is $10t = 5t$

$\Rightarrow 10(-6) = 5(-6) \Rightarrow -60 = -30$, which is not true.

Therefore, the two particles will never collide during their forward motion. Thus, the correct option is (C).

subject: Physics
q: An aircraft moving with uniform speed lets go of a bomb. As the bomb descends from the aircraft,
question: An aircraft moving with uniform speed lets go of a bomb. As the bomb descends from the aircraft,
options: it will continue to stay vertically beneath the aircraft
it will remain vertically below the aircraft only when the aircraft moves horizontally
it will remain vertically below the aircraft only if it is flying at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ to the horizontal
it will slowly fall behind the aircraft if the aircraft travels horizontally
solution:
Let the constant velocity of the aeroplane be $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{a}}$, making an arbitrary angle $\theta$ with the horizontal.

Horizontal component of aeroplane's velocity is $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{ax}}=\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{a}} \cos \theta$

Vertical component of aeroplane's velocity is $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{ay}}=\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{a}} \sin \theta$

Since the aeroplane moves at a constant velocity, its acceleration in both directions is zero ($a_{ax} = 0, a_{ay} = 0$).

The horizontal position of the aeroplane at any time $t$ after releasing the bomb is given by:

$$x_a(t) = v_{ax} \cdot t = (v_a \cos \theta)t$$

At the instant of release, due to inertia of motion, the bomb has the velocity vector of the aeroplane.

Initial horizontal velocity of the bomb is $u_{bx} = v_{ax} = v_a \cos \theta$

Initial vertical velocity of the bomb is $u_{by} = v_{ay} = v_a \sin \theta$

Once released, the bomb experiences no horizontal force (neglecting air resistance), which means its horizontal acceleration is zero ($a_{bx} = 0$). It experiences a downward vertical acceleration due to gravity ($a_{by} = -g$).

The horizontal position of the bomb at any time $t$ during its flight is given by:

$$x_b(t) = u_{bx} \cdot t = (v_a \cos \theta)t$$

Comparing the equations for horizontal position at any time $t$:

$$x_a(t) = x_b(t)$$

Because their horizontal coordinates remain identical at every single instant throughout the flight, the bomb will always stay on the exact same vertical line as the aeroplane. This condition does not depend on the angle $\theta$; it is true whether the plane is flying horizontally ($\theta = 0^\circ$), climbing upward, or diving downward, as long as the aeroplane maintains its constant velocity vector.

Therefore, the bomb will always be vertically below the aeroplane.

Thus, the correct option is (A).

subject: Physics
q: A projectile is launched at time $t=0$ from point $A$ which is at height $h$ above the floor with speed $u$ (in $\mathbf{m ~ s}^{-\mathbf{1}}$ ) and at an angle $\theta=45^{\circ}$ with the floor. It passes through a hoop at $B$ which is 1 m above $A$ and $B$ is the highest point of the trajectory. The horizontal distance between $A$ and $B$ is $d$ meters. The projectile then falls into a basket, hitting the floor at $C$ at a horizontal distance $3 d$ meters from $A$. Find $h$ (in m).
question: A projectile is launched at time $t=0$ from point $A$ which is at height $h$ above the floor with speed $u$ (in $\mathbf{m ~ s}^{-\mathbf{1}}$ ) and at an angle $\theta=45^{\circ}$ with the floor. It passes through a hoop at $B$ which is 1 m above $A$ and $B$ is the highest point of the trajectory. The horizontal distance between $A$ and $B$ is $d$ meters. The projectile then falls into a basket, hitting the floor at $C$ at a horizontal distance $3 d$ meters from $A$. Find $h$ (in m).
solution:
Let point A be the origin (0, 0)

It is given that B is the highest point of the trajectory. At the peak, the vertical velocity becomes zero $\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}=0\right)$.

Initial vertical velocity is $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{u} \sin 45^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{u}}{\sqrt{2}}$

Initial horizontal velocity is $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{u} \cos 45^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{u}}{\sqrt{2}}$

At peak the vertical displacement is $y_{A B}=1 \mathrm{~m}$

Using the third equation of motion for the vertical direction from A to $\mathrm{B}\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}^2=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}}^2+2 \mathrm{as}\right)$ :

$$ 0=\left(\frac{\mathrm{u}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2-2 \mathrm{~g}(1) $$

$$ \frac{\mathrm{u}^2}{2}=2 \mathrm{~g} \Rightarrow \mathrm{u}^2=4 \mathrm{~g} \ldots (i)$$

Since B is the peak, the time taken to reach $\mathrm{B}\left(\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{B}}\right)$ can be found using $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}}+$ at :

$$ 0=\frac{u}{\sqrt{2}}-g\left(t_B\right) \Rightarrow t_B=\frac{u}{g \sqrt{2}} $$

The horizontal distance d is :

$$ d=u_x \cdot t_B=\left(\frac{u}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(\frac{u}{g \sqrt{2}}\right)=\frac{u^2}{2 g} $$

Substituting $\mathrm{u}^2=4 \mathrm{~g}$ from equation (i):

$$ \mathrm{d}=\frac{4 \mathrm{~g}}{2 \mathrm{~g}}=2 \mathrm{~m} $$

Point C is where the projectile hits the floor.

Horizontal displacement from A to C is $\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{C}}=3 \mathrm{~d}=3(2)=6 \mathrm{~m}$

Since it hits the floor and A is at height l , the displacement is $\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{C}}=-\mathrm{l}$.

Using equation of trajectory, $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x} \tan \theta-\frac{\mathrm{gx}^2}{2 \mathrm{u}^2 \cos ^2 \theta}$

$$ -\mathrm{l}=(6) \tan 45^{\circ}-\frac{\mathrm{g}(6)^2}{2(4 \mathrm{~g})\left(\cos 45^{\circ}\right)^2} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $-l=6(1)-\frac{36}{8 \times(1 / \sqrt{2})^2} \Rightarrow-l=6-9$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ -\mathrm{l}=-3 \Rightarrow \mathrm{l}=3 \mathrm{~m} $$

Therefore, the height lis $\mathbf{3} \mathbf{~ m}$.

subject: Physics
q: A projectile is moving at $60 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at its highest point, where it breaks into two equal parts due to an internal explosion. One part moves vertically up at $50 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ with respect to the ground. The other part will move at
question: A projectile is moving at $60 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at its highest point, where it breaks into two equal parts due to an internal explosion. One part moves vertically up at $50 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ with respect to the ground. The other part will move at
solution:
Let the total mass of the projectile be M .

At the highest point of its trajectory, a projectile has only a horizontal component of velocity. Its vertical velocity component is completely zero.

Given that the speed at the highest point is $60 ext{ m/s}$, the velocity vector just before the explosion $(\vec{V}_i)$ is

$$\vec{v}_i = 60\hat{i}\ \text{m/s}$$

Therefore, the initial momentum vector $(\vec{P}_i)$ of the projectile is:

$$\vec{P}_i = M\vec{v}_i = M(60\hat{i}) = 60M\hat{i}$$

The explosion is caused by internal forces. Since no external forces act on the system during the brief duration of the explosion, the total linear momentum of the system must be conserved in all directions.

The projectile splits into two equal parts, so the mass of each fragment is:

$$m_1 = \frac{M}{2};\, m_2 = \frac{M}{2}$$

First fragment $(m_1)$ moves vertically upward at $50\text{ m/s}$ with respect to the ground.

$$\vec{v}_1 = 50\hat{j}\ \text{m/s}$$

Second fragment $(m_2)$ has unknown velocity vector be $\vec{v}_2$:

$$\vec{v}_2 = v_{2x}\hat{i} + v_{2y}\hat{j}$$

The total final momentum vector $(\vec{P}_f)$ of both fragments combined is:

$$\vec{P}_f = m_1\vec{v}_1 + m_2\vec{v}_2$$

$$\vec{P}_f = \left(\frac{M}{2}\right)(50\hat{\jmath}) + \left(\frac{M}{2}\right)\vec{v}_2$$

Using conservation of linear momentum initial momentum equal to final momentum ($\vec{P}_i = \vec{P}_f$):

$$60M\hat{\imath} = \frac{50M}{2}\hat{\jmath} + \frac{M}{2}\vec{v}_2$$

$$60\hat{\imath} = 25\hat{\jmath} + \frac{1}{2}\vec{v}_2$$

$$60\hat{\imath} - 25\hat{\jmath} = \frac{1}{2}\vec{v}_2$$

$$\vec{v}_2 = 2(60\hat{\imath} - 25\hat{\jmath})$$

$$\vec{v}_2 = 120\hat{\imath} - 50\hat{\jmath}\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

The speed of the second fragment is the magnitude of its velocity vector $\vec{v}_2$.

$$v_2 = \sqrt{(120)^2 + (-50)^2}$$

$$v_2 = \sqrt{14400 + 2500}$$

$$v_2 = \sqrt{16900}$$

$$v_2 = 130\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

Therefore, the second part will move at a speed of 130 m/s.

subject: Physics
q: A ball is projected from a point on the ground at a distance 10 m from the foot of a pole. The angle of projection is $45^{\circ}$. The ball just misses the top of the pole and lands on the other side of it at a distance of 24 m from the pole. If the height of the pole is h. Then Find 34h.
question: A ball is projected from a point on the ground at a distance 10 m from the foot of a pole. The angle of projection is $45^{\circ}$. The ball just misses the top of the pole and lands on the other side of it at a distance of 24 m from the pole. If the height of the pole is h. Then Find 34h.
solution:
Range of the projectile is given. $x$ coordinate of a point in its path is given and its $y$ coordinate is being asked. It is a direct question based on trajectory equation in terms of range $(R)$.

Trajectory of the ball is shown. Its range is $R=10+24=34 \mathrm{~m}$. If height of the pole is $h$, then coordinates of its top point $P$ are $(10, h)$ and they must satisfy the trajectory equation.

$\begin{aligned} y & =x \tan \theta\left[1-\left(\frac{x}{R}\right)\right] \\ h & =10 \tan 45^{\circ}\left[1-\left(\frac{10}{34}\right)\right] \\ & =10 \times \frac{24}{34}\end{aligned}$

$$\therefore $$ 34h = 240

subject: Physics
q: A boy can throw a stone upto a maximum heightof 10 m. The maximum horizontal distance that the boy can throw the same stone upto will be
question: A boy can throw a stone upto a maximum heightof 10 m. The maximum horizontal distance that the boy can throw the same stone upto will be
solution:
When the boy throws the stone straight up, it reaches its highest point. This happens when the stone is projected at an angle of $ \theta_{\mathrm{H}_{\max}} = 90^{\circ} $ to the horizontal. The formula for the maximum height is

$$ H_{\max} = \frac{u^2 \sin^2 \theta_{\mathrm{H}_{\max}}}{2g} = \frac{u^2 \sin^2 90^{\circ}}{2g} = \frac{u^2}{2g}. $$

To make the stone go the farthest horizontally, it must be thrown at an angle of $ \theta_{\mathrm{R}_{\max}} = 45^{\circ} $. The formula for the maximum range is

$$ R_{\max} = \frac{u^2 \sin 2\theta_{\mathrm{R}_{\max}}}{g} = \frac{u^2 \sin 90^{\circ}}{g} = \frac{u^2}{g}. $$

We can also write

$$ R_{\max} = 2 \times \frac{u^2}{2g} = 2H_{\max}. $$

Since the maximum height is given as $ H_{\max} = 10 \, \text{m} $, the maximum horizontal distance (range) is

$$ R_{\max} = 2(10) = 20 \, \text{m}. $$

subject: Physics
q: A bullet is shot from the ground with an initial speed of $100\,\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. It hits a vertical steel wall placed 100 m away from the gun. The bullet comes back following the law of reflection, and its speed remains unchanged. After rebounding, it strikes a balloon that is kept vertically above the gun. Find the height of the balloon.
question: A bullet is shot from the ground with an initial speed of $100\,\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. It hits a vertical steel wall placed 100 m away from the gun. The bullet comes back following the law of reflection, and its speed remains unchanged. After rebounding, it strikes a balloon that is kept vertically above the gun. Find the height of the balloon.
solution:
The bullet is fired with a speed of $100\,\mathrm{m/s}$ at an angle of $45^\circ$. When it hits the steel wall and bounces back, it travels as if the wall were a mirror, so the reflected path is the mirror image of the path it would have followed if there had been no wall.

This means the motion from $O \rightarrow C \rightarrow B$ (with reflection) takes the same time as motion from $O \rightarrow C \rightarrow B'$ had the bullet continued straight without hitting the wall.

The total horizontal distance for this imaginary motion is $200\,\mathrm{m}$ (since it travels 100 m forward and would have gone another 100 m beyond the wall if there were no collision).

So, using the horizontal component of velocity $u_x = u\cos45^\circ$:

$$t = \frac{x}{u_x} = \frac{200}{100\cos45^\circ} = 2\sqrt{2}\,\mathrm{s}$$

The height of the balloon is the vertical position of the bullet after this time.

Using $u_y = u\sin45^\circ = \frac{100}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$h = u_y t - \frac{1}{2}gt^2$$

Substitute values:

$$h = \frac{100}{\sqrt{2}} \times 2\sqrt{2} - \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times (2\sqrt{2})^2$$

$$h = 200 - 40 = 160\,\mathrm{m}$$
